### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical for search/preview):**
> Your joints ache. Your doctor says it's "just arthritis" and hands you another painkiller. But what if the real cause isn't in your joints at all — it's leaking from your gut?

**Full description:**
> Your joints ache. Your doctor says it's "just arthritis" and hands you another painkiller. But what if the real cause isn't in your joints at all — it's leaking from your gut?

> After 60, the lining of your intestine can become more permeable — a condition researchers call "intestinal permeability" or "leaky gut." When this happens, bacterial fragments called lipopolysaccharides (LPS) escape into your bloodstream and trigger inflammation far from the gut — especially in your joints.

> In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains:
> - What leaky gut actually is (and what it isn't)
> - The specific mechanism by which gut-derived inflammation targets joints
> - 3 lab tests that go beyond the standard panel (zonulin, LPS antibodies, calprotectin)
> - What to discuss with your doctor — including word-for-word advocacy scripts
> - The difference between managing joint pain and addressing its hidden source

> ⚠️ This video does not diagnose leaky gut or recommend stopping any medication. Always discuss testing and treatment with your physician.

> 📌 REFERENCES:
> - Fasano, A. (2012). "Zonulin, regulation of tight junctions, and autoimmune diseases." Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences.
> - Mu, Q. et al. (2017). "Leakage of gut microbial markers is associated with autoimmunity in siblings of children with autism." Microbiome.

> 🔗 RELATED VIDEOS:
> - VID-010: What Microplastics Do to Your Gut After 60
> - VID-020: Your Iron Levels Look Normal After 60 — But Your Cells Are Starving
> - VID-022: Your Kidney Test Looks Normal After 60 — But Your Kidneys Are Failing

## Video 2: I114 — Doctor's Scale Is Lying (VID-003)

**Topic:** Body Composition / What Your Scale Doesn't Tell You
**Production Target:** VID-033
**Priority Score:** 21 (URGENT)
**Pillar:** Body Composition / Diagnostic Gap

### Title Variations (Top 3)

| # | Title | Chars | Pattern Used |
|---|-------|-------|-------------|
| T1 | Your Scale Looks Normal After 60 — But Your Body Is Changing | 58 | P022: "Your [Metric] Looks Normal After [Age] — But Your [Body Part] Are [Failing Verb]" |
| T2 | Why the Scale Is Wrong After 60 — The Body Composition Secret | 60 | P013: "Why Your [Body Part/Function] [Symptom] After 60 — The [Hidden Mechanism] Secret" |
| T3 | 3 Things Your Scale Hides After 60 | 36 | P003: "Your Body Is [Missing/Hiding] [Number] [Critical Thing] After 60" |

**Recommended:** T1 — directly applies the "looks normal but..." diagnostic gap pattern (P022, 19 score) applied to body composition. The word "scale" creates immediate relatability — everyone weighs themselves.

### SEO Description

**First 2 sentences (critical for search/preview):**
> You step on the scale and the number looks fine. But what if that number is hiding the most dangerous change happening inside your body after 60? Your weight can stay the same while your muscle disappears and fat accumulates — silently.

**Full description:**
> You step on the scale and the number looks fine. But what if that number is hiding the most dangerous change happening inside your body after 60?

> After 60, a process called "sarcopenia" — age-related muscle loss — can cause your body composition to shift dramatically even while your weight stays exactly the same. Your muscles shrink, visceral fat increases, and the scale tells you... nothing changed.

> In this video, Dr. Victor Kane explains:
> - Why BMI and scale weight fail after 60
> - The 3 body composition changes the scale can't see (muscle, visceral fat, bone density)
> - Simple at-home assessments: the grip strength test, the sit-to-stand test, the waist-to-hip ratio
> - What tests to ask your doctor for (DEXA, bioelectrical impedance, waist circumference tracking)
> - How to track progress without the scale

> ⚠️ This video provides general health information. It does not diagnose sarcopenia or any metabolic condition. Always consult your physician about body composition changes.

> 📌 REFERENCES:
> - Cruz-Jentoft, A.J. et al. (2019). "Sarcopenia: revised European consensus on definition and diagnosis." Age and Ageing.
> - Batsis, J.A. & Villareal, D.T. (2018). "Sarcopenia in older adults: a review." Current Obesity Reports.

> 🔗 RELATED VIDEOS:

> - VID-026: The 3 AM Liver Dump — Why Your Blood Sugar Spikes Overnight
